refutationAttempt: The catalog title/year/label/artist fields match the embedded MB release, so I tried to read the prose as merely thin or as an opinion about the same record.
decision: Fails: the prose self-identifies the album as 'Haydn, Hummel, L. Mozart: Trumpet Concertos' with the National Philharmonic Orchestra, but the MB release is titled 'Baroque Music for Trumpet' and credits the English Chamber Orchestra. The prose is the verbatim lede of a different Marsalis classical album, not this one.
contradictingQuote: English Chamber Orchestra
specificBadClaim: Haydn, Hummel, L. Mozart: Trumpet Concertos is a studio album of trumpet concertos by Joseph Haydn, Leopold Mozart and Johann Nepomuk Hummel, performed by Wynton Marsalis with the National Philharmonic Orchestra under the direction of Raymond Leppard

refutationAttempt: Tried to treat the prose as right-entity drift, since the trailing bio sentence is generically true of Art Pepper and the keyTracks match this release.
decision: Fails: the prose opens by naming and describing a different album ('Modern Art', Intro label, 1956-57, reissued 1988), while the MB release under verification is 'Straight Life - The Savoy Sessions' on Savoy Jazz. Wholesale about another work.
contradictingQuote: Straight Life - The Savoy Sessions
specificBadClaim: Modern Art is an album by saxophonist Art Pepper featuring sessions recorded in late 1956 and early 1957 originally released on the Intro label. The album was reissued on CD on Blue Note Records with bonus tracks as Modern Art: The Complete Art Pepper Aladdin Recordings Volume 2 in 1988.

refutationAttempt: Tried to read it as same-leader editorial drift, since Michael Brecker is genuinely tied to the Brecker Brothers.
decision: Fails: the entire prose describes 'Out of the Loop' (Brecker Brothers, GRP, 1994), a distinct album, while the MB release under verification is 'Hearts and Numbers' (1985, Hip Pocket). Wholesale about another work.
contradictingQuote: Hearts and Numbers
specificBadClaim: Out of the Loop is the seventh and final studio album by the Brecker Brothers that was released by GRP Records in 1994. In 1995 the album won the brothers two Grammy Awards for Best Contemporary Jazz Performance and Best Instrumental Composition.

refutationAttempt: Tried to argue the duo framing is loosely true of Blanchard's era, so the prose might just be poorly scoped about the same release.
decision: Fails: the prose is the verbatim lede of 'Black Pearl' (1988), naming a different title and year, while the MB release under verification is 'Crystal Stair' (1987, Columbia). Wholesale about another work.
contradictingQuote: Black Pearl is an album by the American jazz duo Harrison/Blanchard, released in 1988. It was their final album together
specificBadClaim: Black Pearl is an album by the American jazz duo Harrison/Blanchard, released in 1988. It was their final album together; Terence Blanchard began his long partnership with the director Spike Lee on School Daze, released the same year. The duo supported the album with a North American tour.

refutationAttempt: Tried hardest here: the prose is about Louis Armstrong, who IS this album's artist, so it could be defended as right-artist content rather than a mismatch.
decision: Fails: the prose is wholesale a person-biography of Louis Armstrong (lifespan, nicknames, career-spanning accolades) copied from his artist page, not a description of the album 'Satchmo'. Per the distinguishing rule, prose wholesale about a person while the item is an album is an entity_mismatch.
contradictingQuote: Louis Daniel Armstrong (August 4, 1901 – July 6, 1971), nicknamed "Satchmo", "Satch", and "Pops", was an American jazz and blues trumpeter and vocalist.
specificBadClaim: Louis Daniel Armstrong, nicknamed "Satchmo", "Satch", and "Pops", was an American jazz and blues trumpeter and vocalist.

refutationAttempt: Tried to read the prose as a generic Tom Harrell note loosely attached to the right record.
decision: Fails: the prose is the verbatim lede of 'Sail Away' (1989, Contemporary Records), a different title/year/label, while the MB release under verification is 'Stories' (1988). Wholesale about another work.
contradictingQuote: Sail Away is an album by the American musician Tom Harrell. It was released in 1989 via Contemporary Records.
specificBadClaim: Sail Away is an album by the American musician Tom Harrell. It was released in 1989 via Contemporary Records. The title track became a jazz standard. The album was reissued in 2003 with two bonus tracks.

refutationAttempt: Tried to defend it via the shared Warner Bros./Sanborn link, treating 'Double Vision' framing as same-artist drift.
decision: Fails: the prose describes 'Double Vision' (1986, Bob James and David Sanborn), but the MB release and the matched, correct wiki page are the self-titled 'David Sanborn' album by Sanborn alone. The prose names a different collaborative album.
contradictingQuote: Sanborn is an album by jazz saxophonist David Sanborn, released by Warner Bros. in 1976.
specificBadClaim: Double Vision is a 1986 album by Bob James and David Sanborn.

refutationAttempt: Tried hard: the second sentence correctly cites 'Dear Dessa', Enja, and 1991, so the prose is partly about the right album and might be salvaged as a single wrong fact.
decision: Fails: the album's primary identity statement names it as 'Night Song ... released via Clarity Recordings in 1997', the verbatim lede of a different Arthur Blythe album, while MB confirms the item is 'Hipmotism' on enja, firstReleaseDate 1991. A wrong album name with a copied lede is a mismatch, not one wrong fact on the right entity.
contradictingQuote: Night Song is an album by the saxophonist Arthur Blythe, released via Clarity Recordings in 1997.
specificBadClaim: Night Song is an album by the saxophonist Arthur Blythe, released via Clarity Recordings in 1997.

refutationAttempt: Tried to treat it as same-artist drift, since keyTracks match this MB compilation and Gillespie is the right leader.
decision: Fails: the prose is the verbatim lede of 'Dizzy Gillespie and the Double Six of Paris' (1963), a different album with a different program, while MB confirms the item is the 1992 Movieplay 'A Jazz Hour With Dizzy Gillespie'. Wholesale about another work.
contradictingQuote: Dizzy Gillespie and the Double Six of Paris is a 1963 studio album collaboration between Dizzy Gillespie and Les Double Six
specificBadClaim: Dizzy Gillespie and the Double Six of Paris is a 1963 studio album collaboration between Dizzy Gillespie and Les Double Six, also known as the Double Six of Paris

refutationAttempt: No plausible refutation: I checked whether the surname overlap could make this same-artist drift.
decision: Fails: the prose is the biography of Post Malone (pop/hip-hop, born 1995), a completely different person from the jazz guitarist Russell Malone whose 1992 Columbia album MB confirms. Wholesale about a different person.
contradictingQuote: Austin Richard Post (born July 4, 1995), known professionally as Post Malone, is an American singer, rapper, songwriter, record producer, and actor.
specificBadClaim: Austin Richard Post, known professionally as Post Malone, is an American singer, rapper, songwriter, record producer, and actor.

refutationAttempt: Tried hard: title/year/label match the MB release and the named tracks ('Blue Rondo a la Turk', 'Premiere rhapsodie') are genuinely on it, so 'bebop setting' could be dismissed as mere editorial framing.
decision: Fails: the prose presents the album as a Charlie Parker bebop date, but the MB release is a multi-credit clarinet recital (Brubeck, Debussy, Poulenc, Honegger, Stravinsky, Parker plus clarinettist Karl-Heinz Steffens and pianist David Gazarov) whose tracklist is classical clarinet sonatas. Parker is one composer credit among many, so framing the entity as his bebop album misidentifies it.
contradictingQuote: Clarinet Sonata, FP184: I. Allegro tristemente
specificBadClaim: A 1997 Tudor date, Blue Rondo finds Charlie Parker in a bebop setting.

refutationAttempt: Tried to defend 'cool jazz tradition' as editorial opinion attached to a release whose title/year/label match.
decision: Fails: the prose presents the album as Dave Brubeck's cool-jazz album, but the same MB release is a multi-credit clarinet recital (Brubeck appears only as one of fifteen credits among Debussy, Poulenc, Stravinsky, clarinettist Karl-Heinz Steffens, etc.) with a classical clarinet tracklist. Misidentifies the entity as a single-artist Brubeck record.
contradictingQuote: Clarinet Sonata, FP184: I. Allegro tristemente
specificBadClaim: Pianist Dave Brubeck's 1997 album Blue Rondo draws from the cool jazz tradition, released on Tudor.

refutationAttempt: Tried to read it as same-artist drift, since Terence Blanchard is the right leader and keyTracks match this MB release.
decision: Fails: the prose is the verbatim lede of Blanchard's 1991 self-titled debut on Columbia, a different title/year/label, while MB confirms the item is 'Jazz in Film' (1999, Sony Classical). Wholesale about another work.
contradictingQuote: Terence Blanchard is an album by American jazz trumpeter Terence Blanchard. This is his debut full-length album as a leader. The record was released on June 18, 1991, via Columbia Records.
specificBadClaim: Terence Blanchard is an album by American jazz trumpeter Terence Blanchard. This is his debut full-length album as a leader. The record was released on June 18, 1991, via Columbia Records.

refutationAttempt: No MB tracklist exists for our album, so I tried to argue the mismatch is unverifiable and the prose could loosely concern the same Katrina-era Blanchard project.

refutationAttempt: Tried to treat it as series drift, since both are Book of Angels volumes with Zorn as composer and most keyTracks match this MB release.

refutationAttempt: Tried hardest here: same artist and same source recordings, so the box set and the single disc could be argued to be the same Library of Congress entity with a wrong format detail.
decision: Fails: the wiki extract itself draws the line, stating that the single-disc 'The Library of Congress Recordings by Alan Lomax' (our item, MB-confirmed) is a separate 2007 highlights release derived from the 8-CD box set the prose describes. The prose's hard facts (8 CDs, 128 tracks, 2005, two Grammys) belong to the distinct box set.
contradictingQuote: In 2007, Rounder released Jelly Roll Morton: The Library of Congress Recordings by Alan Lomax, a single disc consisting of selected highlights from the box set.
specificBadClaim: Jelly Roll Morton: The Complete Library of Congress Recordings is a 2005 box set of recordings from jazz pioneer Jelly Roll Morton. The set spans 128 tracks over eight CDs.

refutationAttempt: Tried to treat 'Ballads' as same-artist/same-label drift, since both are Derek Bailey solo guitar albums on Tzadik and keyTracks match this MB release.

contradictingQuote: Ballads is a solo studio album by English guitarist Derek Bailey. It was released on 23 April 2002, through Tzadik.
specificBadClaim: Ballads is a solo studio album by English guitarist Derek Bailey. It was released on 23 April 2002, through Tzadik.

refutationAttempt: Tried to treat it as same-artist Mingus drift, since title/year/label fields and keyTracks all match this MB release.

refutationAttempt: Tried to read the Roditi material as drift, since the trailing sentence correctly names Rubalcaba and the title 'XXI Century' matches.

Wrong artist, instrument, and year.
